bibliographic_data.title: Alice Meets Bob: A Comparative Usability Study of Wireless Device Pairing Methods for a "Two-User" Setting
bibliographic_data.abstract: When users want to establish wireless communication between/among their devices, the channel has to be bootstrapped first. To prevent any malicious control of or eavesdropping over the communication, the channel is desired to be authenticated and confidential. The process of setting up a secure communication channel between two previously unassociated devices is referred to as 'Secure Device Pairing'. When there is no prior security context, e.g., shared secrets, common key servers or public key certificates, device pairing requires user involvement into the process. The idea usually involves leveraging an auxiliary human-perceptible channel to authenticate the data exchanged over the insecure wireless channel. We observe that the focus of prior research has mostly been limited to pairing scenarios where a single user controls both the devices. In this paper, we consider more general and emerging 'two-user' scenarios, where two different users establish pairing between their respective devices. Although a number of pairing methods exist in the literature, only a handful of those are applicable to the two-user setting. We present the first study to identify the methods practical for two-user pairing scenarios, and comparatively evaluate the usability of these methods. Our results identify methods best-suited for users, in terms of efficiency, error-tolerance and of course, usability. Our work sheds light on the applicability and usability of pairing methods for emerging two-user scenarios, a topic largely ignored so far.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper identifies and evaluates methods practical for secure device pairing in a 'two-user' setting, where two different users establish pairing between their respective devices.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors implemented selected methods using a common software platform and conducted a comprehensive field study focusing on both usability and security in a two-user setting.
outcomes: Identified methods best-suited for two-user scenarios
Compared the usability of these methods
core_content.full_text_markdown: In this paper, we address the challenge of secure device pairing in 'two-user' settings. Prior research has mostly focused on single-user scenarios where a single user controls both devices. We identify and evaluate methods practical for two-user pairing scenarios, comparing their usability. Our study highlights the need to consider security and privacy implications when applying existing single-user methods in a two-user context.
